answer_detailed: An oxymoron is a figure of speech that juxtaposes two contradictory terms for rhetorical effect. Examples include 'deafening silence,' 'bittersweet,' 'living dead,' 'pretty ugly,' and 'act naturally.'

canonical_question: What language has the most words?
answer_short: English, with over 170,000 words in current use
answer_detailed: English has the largest vocabulary of any language, with the Oxford English Dictionary containing over 170,000 words in current use and over 600,000 total entries including archaic words. This is partly due to English borrowing extensively from other languages.

answer_short: Giving human qualities to non-human things
answer_detailed: Personification is a figure of speech that attributes human characteristics to non-human things, animals, or abstract concepts. Examples include 'the wind whispered,' 'time flies,' and 'the sun smiled down on them.'

answer_short: Marks used in writing to separate sentences and clarify meaning
answer_detailed: Punctuation consists of marks used in writing to separate sentences, clauses, and phrases, and to clarify meaning. Common marks include periods, commas, semicolons, colons, question marks, exclamation marks, quotation marks, and apostrophes.

answer_short: A word formed from the initial letters of other words
answer_detailed: An acronym is a word or name formed from the initial components (usually letters) of a longer name or phrase. Examples include NASA (National Aeronautics and Space Administration), SCUBA (Self-Contained Underwater Breathing Apparatus), and NATO.

answer_short: A distinct section of writing dealing with a single theme
answer_detailed: A paragraph is a distinct section of writing that deals with a single topic or idea. It typically begins with a topic sentence, followed by supporting sentences, and may end with a concluding sentence. Paragraphs organize writing into logical units.

answer_short: Words that sound the same but have different meanings and spellings
answer_detailed: Homophones are words that are pronounced the same but differ in meaning and often in spelling. Examples include there/their/they're, to/too/two, flour/flower, and bare/bear. They are a common source of spelling errors.

canonical_question: What is the origin of the word 'computer'?
answer_short: From Latin 'computare' meaning to calculate or reckon
answer_detailed: The word 'computer' comes from the Latin 'computare,' meaning to calculate. Originally it referred to a person who performed calculations. The first known use for a machine was in 1613. The modern electronic meaning dates to the 1940s.

answer_short: A short, well-known saying expressing a general truth
answer_detailed: A proverb is a short, pithy saying that expresses a traditionally held truth or piece of advice. Examples include 'Actions speak louder than words,' 'A stitch in time saves nine,' and 'The early bird catches the worm.'

answer_short: The largest internal organ, performing over 500 functions
answer_detailed: The liver is the largest internal organ in the human body, weighing about 1.5 kg. It performs over 500 functions including filtering blood, producing bile for digestion, metabolizing drugs, storing glycogen, and producing proteins essential for blood clotting.

answer_short: Organs responsible for gas exchange during breathing
answer_detailed: The lungs are the primary organs of the respiratory system. They extract oxygen from inhaled air and release carbon dioxide during exhalation. An adult takes about 12-20 breaths per minute, processing about 11,000 liters of air daily.

answer_short: A vitamin essential for calcium absorption and bone health
answer_detailed: Vitamin D is essential for calcium absorption, bone health, and immune function. The body produces it when skin is exposed to sunlight. Deficiency causes rickets in children and osteomalacia in adults. Good sources include fatty fish, fortified milk, and sunlight.

answer_short: Units of energy in food, needed to fuel body functions
answer_detailed: A calorie is a unit of energy. In nutrition, 'calorie' typically means kilocalorie (kcal). An average adult needs about 2,000-2,500 calories per day. Carbohydrates and proteins provide 4 kcal per gram; fats provide 9 kcal per gram.

answer_short: A group of diseases involving abnormal cell growth
answer_detailed: Cancer is a group of diseases characterized by uncontrolled cell growth and spread to other parts of the body (metastasis). There are over 100 types. Major causes include genetic mutations, smoking, radiation, and certain viruses. Treatment includes surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation therapy.

answer_short: A contagious respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses
answer_detailed: Influenza (flu) is a contagious respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses. Symptoms include fever, cough, body aches, and fatigue. Annual flu epidemics cause 3-5 million severe cases worldwide. The 1918 Spanish Flu pandemic killed an estimated 50-100 million people.

answer_short: The organ system that moves blood through the body
answer_detailed: The circulatory system consists of the heart, blood vessels (arteries, veins, capillaries), and blood. It transports oxygen, nutrients, and hormones to cells and removes waste products. An adult body contains about 5 liters of blood and 100,000 km of blood vessels.
aliases: how does the circulatory system work

answer_short: The body's electrical wiring that controls all functions
answer_detailed: The nervous system is the body's communication network, consisting of the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) and peripheral nervous system. It transmits signals between different body parts using neurons, controlling everything from movement to thought.
aliases: how does the nervous system work

answer_short: Essential macronutrients made of amino acids for body repair and growth
answer_detailed: Dietary proteins are macronutrients essential for building and repairing tissues, making enzymes and hormones, and supporting immune function. Good sources include meat, fish, eggs, dairy, legumes, and nuts. Adults need about 0.8g of protein per kg of body weight daily.
aliases: how much protein do I need

answer_short: Abnormally high blood pressure (above 130/80 mmHg)
answer_detailed: Hypertension (high blood pressure) is when blood pressure is consistently above 130/80 mmHg. It is a major risk factor for heart disease, stroke, and kidney disease. It affects about 1.3 billion people worldwide. Treatment includes lifestyle changes and medication.

answer_short: A chronic condition causing airway inflammation and breathing difficulty
answer_detailed: Asthma is a chronic respiratory condition where airways narrow, swell, and produce extra mucus, making breathing difficult. Triggers include allergens, exercise, cold air, and stress. It affects about 300 million people worldwide and is managed with inhalers.

answer_short: Tissues that contract to produce movement
answer_detailed: Muscles are soft tissues that contract to produce force and motion. The human body has over 600 muscles in three types: skeletal (voluntary movement), cardiac (heart), and smooth (internal organs). Muscles make up about 40% of body weight.

answer_short: The body's primary source of energy from sugars, starches, and fiber
answer_detailed: Carbohydrates are the body's main energy source, found in foods like grains, fruits, and vegetables. They are classified as simple (sugars) and complex (starches and fiber). Each gram provides 4 calories. They are broken down into glucose for energy.

answer_short: Essential macronutrients providing concentrated energy at 9 calories per gram
answer_detailed: Dietary fats are macronutrients that provide concentrated energy (9 kcal/g), absorb fat-soluble vitamins, insulate organs, and build cell membranes. Types include saturated, unsaturated (mono and poly), and trans fats. Healthy sources include olive oil, nuts, and fish.

answer_short: Organs that filter blood and produce urine
answer_detailed: The kidneys are bean-shaped organs that filter about 200 liters of blood daily, removing waste products and excess fluid to produce urine. Each kidney contains about 1 million nephrons. They also regulate blood pressure, electrolyte balance, and red blood cell production.

answer_short: The organ system that breaks down food and absorbs nutrients
answer_detailed: The digestive system breaks down food into nutrients the body can absorb. It includes the mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, pancreas, and gallbladder. The entire digestive tract is about 9 meters long.

answer_short: A mosquito-borne disease caused by Plasmodium parasites
answer_detailed: Malaria is a life-threatening disease caused by Plasmodium parasites transmitted through infected Anopheles mosquito bites. Symptoms include fever, chills, and flu-like illness. It kills over 600,000 people annually, mostly children in sub-Saharan Africa.

answer_short: A respiratory illness caused by the SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus
answer_detailed: COVID-19 is an infectious disease caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus, first identified in Wuhan, China, in December 2019. It caused a global pandemic with over 6 million deaths. Symptoms range from mild respiratory issues to severe pneumonia. Vaccines were developed in record time.

canonical_question: How many ounces in a pound?
answer_short: 16 ounces
answer_detailed: There are 16 ounces (oz) in one pound (lb) in the avoirdupois system used in the US and UK. One pound equals approximately 453.6 grams in the metric system.
aliases: how many oz in a lb

canonical_question: How many cups in a gallon?
answer_short: 16 cups
answer_detailed: There are 16 cups in one US gallon, 8 cups in a half gallon, and 4 cups in a quart. One US gallon equals approximately 3.785 liters. The imperial gallon (used in UK) is larger at about 4.546 liters.

canonical_question: How many centimeters in an inch?
answer_short: 2.54 centimeters
answer_detailed: One inch equals exactly 2.54 centimeters. This conversion was standardized internationally in 1959. An inch is a unit of length in the imperial system, while centimeters are metric.

answer_short: 32 degrees Fahrenheit
answer_detailed: 0 degrees Celsius equals 32 degrees Fahrenheit. The conversion formula is F = (C x 9/5) + 32. At -40 degrees, both scales read the same. Water boils at 100C (212F) and freezes at 0C (32F).

canonical_question: What is the tallest building in the world?
answer_short: Burj Khalifa in Dubai at 828 m (2,717 ft)
answer_detailed: The Burj Khalifa in Dubai, UAE, is the tallest building in the world at 828 metres (2,717 feet) with 163 floors. It was completed in 2010. The next tallest is the Merdeka 118 in Kuala Lumpur at 678.9 metres.

canonical_question: What is the deepest lake in the world?
answer_short: Lake Baikal in Russia at 1,642 m deep
answer_detailed: Lake Baikal in Siberia, Russia, is the deepest lake in the world at 1,642 metres (5,387 feet) and the largest freshwater lake by volume. It contains about 20% of the world's unfrozen surface fresh water and is estimated to be 25 million years old.

canonical_question: What is the largest animal in the world?
answer_short: The blue whale, up to 30 m long and 200 tonnes
answer_detailed: The blue whale is the largest animal ever known to have existed, reaching lengths of up to 30 metres and weights of up to 200 tonnes. Its heart is the size of a small car, and its tongue can weigh as much as an elephant.

canonical_question: What is a light year in kilometers?
answer_short: Approximately 9.461 trillion km
answer_detailed: A light year is approximately 9.461 trillion kilometres (9.461 x 10^12 km). It is the distance that light travels in one Julian year in vacuum. It is used to express distances between stars and galaxies.
aliases: how far is a light year

answer_short: Deoxyribonucleic acid
answer_detailed: DNA stands for deoxyribonucleic acid. It is the molecule that carries genetic instructions for the development, functioning, growth, and reproduction of all known organisms. Its double-helix structure was discovered by Watson and Crick in 1953.

answer_short: National Aeronautics and Space Administration
answer_detailed: NASA stands for the National Aeronautics and Space Administration. It is the US government agency responsible for the civilian space program, aeronautics research, and space science. Founded in 1958, it conducted the Apollo Moon landings and operates the ISS.

canonical_question: What is the hottest temperature ever recorded?
answer_short: 56.7 C (134 F) in Death Valley, California, 1913
answer_detailed: The highest temperature ever reliably recorded on Earth is 56.7 degrees Celsius (134 degrees Fahrenheit) at Furnace Creek Ranch in Death Valley, California, on July 10, 1913.

canonical_question: What is the coldest temperature ever recorded?
answer_short: -89.2 C (-128.6 F) at Vostok Station, Antarctica, 1983
answer_detailed: The lowest naturally occurring temperature recorded on Earth is -89.2 degrees Celsius (-128.6 degrees Fahrenheit) at the Soviet Vostok Station in Antarctica on July 21, 1983.

answer_short: A unit of digital information equal to 8 bits
answer_detailed: A byte is a unit of digital information consisting of 8 bits. It is the standard unit for measuring data storage. A kilobyte (KB) is 1,024 bytes, a megabyte (MB) is 1,024 KB, a gigabyte (GB) is 1,024 MB, and a terabyte (TB) is 1,024 GB.
aliases: how many bits in a byte

canonical_question: How many seconds in a day?
answer_short: 86,400 seconds
answer_detailed: There are 86,400 seconds in a day. This is calculated as 24 hours times 60 minutes times 60 seconds (24 x 60 x 60 = 86,400). A year has about 31,536,000 seconds.

canonical_question: How many days in a year?
answer_short: 365 days (366 in a leap year)
answer_detailed: A regular year has 365 days. A leap year, occurring every 4 years (with exceptions for centuries not divisible by 400), has 366 days. The extra day is added as February 29. The mean tropical year is approximately 365.2422 days.

answer_short: 10,000 square metres (about 2.47 acres)
answer_detailed: A hectare is a metric unit of area equal to 10,000 square metres or 100 metres by 100 metres. It equals approximately 2.47 acres. Hectares are commonly used worldwide for measuring land area, especially in agriculture.

canonical_question: What is the most common element in the universe?
answer_short: Hydrogen (about 75% of all normal matter)
answer_detailed: Hydrogen is the most abundant element in the universe, making up about 75% of all normal matter by mass. Helium is second at about 24%. Together they account for about 99% of all atoms. Hydrogen is the fuel for stars through nuclear fusion.
aliases: what is the universe made of

answer_short: Approximately 4.54 billion years
answer_detailed: Earth is approximately 4.54 billion years old, determined by radiometric dating of meteorite material. The oldest known minerals are zircon crystals from Australia dated to 4.4 billion years. Life first appeared about 3.5-4 billion years ago.

answer_short: Potential of hydrogen (or power of hydrogen)
answer_detailed: pH stands for 'potential of hydrogen' or 'power of hydrogen.' It was introduced by Soren Sorensen in 1909 while working at the Carlsberg Laboratory. The 'p' stands for the German word 'Potenz' (power) and 'H' is for hydrogen.

answer_short: 1,852 metres (about 1.15 statute miles)
answer_detailed: A nautical mile is 1,852 metres (6,076 feet), approximately 1.15 statute miles. It is based on one minute of arc of latitude along any meridian. It is the standard unit of distance for maritime and air navigation.
aliases: how long is a nautical mile

canonical_question: What is the longest river in Europe?
answer_short: The Volga River at about 3,530 km
answer_detailed: The Volga River is the longest river in Europe at approximately 3,530 km. It flows through central Russia into the Caspian Sea. It is also the longest river in the world that flows into an inland body of water rather than an ocean.

canonical_question: What is the smallest bone in the human body?
answer_short: The stapes (stirrup bone) in the middle ear
answer_detailed: The stapes (stirrup bone) is the smallest bone in the human body, measuring about 3 mm. Located in the middle ear, it transmits sound vibrations from the incus to the inner ear. It is one of three tiny bones (ossicles) in the middle ear.

answer_short: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization
answer_detailed: UNESCO stands for the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization. Founded in 1945 with headquarters in Paris, it promotes international collaboration in education, science, culture, and communication, and designates World Heritage Sites.

canonical_question: How many miles in a kilometer?
answer_short: Approximately 0.621 miles
answer_detailed: One kilometer equals approximately 0.621 miles, or conversely, one mile equals approximately 1.609 kilometers. The mile is used in the US, UK, and a few other countries, while the kilometre is the standard unit worldwide.

canonical_question: What is the hardest natural substance?
answer_short: Diamond
answer_detailed: Diamond is the hardest known natural substance, rated 10 on the Mohs scale of mineral hardness. It is a form of carbon where atoms are arranged in a crystal structure. Diamonds are used in jewelry, cutting tools, and industrial applications.

canonical_question: What is the speed of the fastest human?
answer_short: 44.72 km/h (27.78 mph) by Usain Bolt
answer_detailed: The fastest speed ever recorded by a human is 44.72 km/h (27.78 mph), achieved by Usain Bolt during his world record 100m sprint of 9.58 seconds in 2009 at the World Championships in Berlin. This peak speed occurred between the 60m and 80m marks.

answer_short: A unit measuring the intensity of sound
answer_detailed: A decibel (dB) is a logarithmic unit used to measure sound intensity. Normal conversation is about 60 dB, a whisper is about 30 dB, and sounds above 85 dB can cause hearing damage with prolonged exposure. The threshold of pain is about 130 dB.

canonical_question: How many tablespoons in a cup?
answer_short: 16 tablespoons
answer_detailed: There are 16 tablespoons in one US cup. One tablespoon equals 3 teaspoons or approximately 14.8 milliliters. One US cup equals approximately 237 milliliters. These measurements are essential in cooking and baking.

canonical_question: What is the most spoken religion in the world?
answer_short: Christianity with about 2.4 billion followers
answer_detailed: Christianity is the world's largest religion with approximately 2.4 billion adherents (about 31% of the global population). Islam is second with about 1.9 billion (25%). Hinduism is third with about 1.2 billion (15%). Buddhism has about 500 million followers.

answer_short: World Wide Web
answer_detailed: WWW stands for World Wide Web, the information system on the internet where documents and resources are identified by URLs and interconnected by hypertext links. It was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989 at CERN.

canonical_question: What is the largest desert in the world?
answer_short: Antarctica (cold desert) or Sahara (hot desert)
answer_detailed: Antarctica is technically the largest desert in the world at 14.2 million km2, as a desert is defined by low precipitation, not heat. The Sahara is the largest hot desert at about 9.2 million km2. The Arctic is the second largest cold desert.

answer_short: A unit of energy equal to 4.184 joules
answer_detailed: A calorie is a unit of energy. One small calorie (cal) is the energy needed to raise the temperature of 1 gram of water by 1 degree Celsius. In nutrition, Calorie (kcal) equals 1,000 small calories. One kcal equals 4,184 joules.

canonical_question: How many elements are on the periodic table?
answer_short: 118 confirmed elements
answer_detailed: There are 118 confirmed elements on the periodic table. The most recently confirmed are nihonium (113), moscovium (115), tennessine (117), and oganesson (118), officially named in 2016. Of these, 94 occur naturally on Earth.

canonical_question: How many liters in a gallon?
answer_short: Approximately 3.785 liters (US gallon)
answer_detailed: One US gallon equals approximately 3.785 liters. One imperial gallon (UK) equals approximately 4.546 liters. The US gallon is used in the United States while the imperial gallon is used in some Commonwealth countries.

answer_short: Ancient trade route network connecting East Asia and Europe
answer_detailed: The Silk Road was a network of trade routes connecting East Asia with Europe and the Middle East from about the 2nd century BC to the 18th century. It facilitated trade in silk, spices, precious metals, and ideas including religion, technology, and disease.

answer_short: An intellectual movement in 17th-18th century Europe emphasizing reason
answer_detailed: The Enlightenment (Age of Reason) was an intellectual and philosophical movement in 17th-18th century Europe that emphasized reason, science, and individual rights over tradition and religious authority. Key figures include Voltaire, Locke, Rousseau, and Kant.

answer_short: A war between North and South Korea from 1950 to 1953
answer_detailed: The Korean War (1950-1953) began when North Korea invaded South Korea on June 25, 1950. The UN, led by the US, supported South Korea while China supported the North. An armistice was signed in 1953 but no peace treaty was ever signed.

canonical_question: When was the printing of the Gutenberg Bible?
answer_detailed: The Gutenberg Bible was printed around 1455 by Johannes Gutenberg in Mainz, Germany. It was the first major book printed using movable metal type in Europe. About 180 copies were printed, of which 49 survive today. It revolutionized the spread of knowledge.
aliases: when was the Gutenberg Bible printed

answer_short: A Mesoamerican civilization in central Mexico from 1300-1521
answer_detailed: The Aztec Empire flourished in central Mexico from the early 14th century until the Spanish conquest in 1521. Their capital Tenochtitlan (now Mexico City) was one of the largest cities in the world with over 200,000 inhabitants. They were known for elaborate temples and a complex calendar.

canonical_question: When was slavery abolished in the British Empire?
answer_short: 1833 (Slavery Abolition Act)
answer_detailed: The Slavery Abolition Act of 1833 abolished slavery throughout most of the British Empire, effective August 1, 1834. The abolitionist movement, led by figures like William Wilberforce, had campaigned for decades. The slave trade had been banned earlier in 1807.
aliases: when was slavery abolished in Britain

answer_short: A severe worldwide economic downturn from 1929 to late 1930s
answer_detailed: The Great Depression was the worst economic downturn in industrialized world history, beginning with the US stock market crash on October 29, 1929 (Black Tuesday). At its worst, unemployment in the US reached 25%. It lasted until the late 1930s.

canonical_question: What is the capital of Turkey?
answer_short: Ankara
answer_detailed: Ankara is the capital of Turkey with a population of about 5.7 million. It replaced Istanbul as the capital in 1923 when the Republic of Turkey was established by Mustafa Kemal Ataturk. It is located in central Anatolia.

canonical_question: What is the capital of Argentina?
answer_short: Buenos Aires
answer_detailed: Buenos Aires is the capital of Argentina and its largest city with about 3 million people in the city proper and 15 million in the metropolitan area. It is known for its European-style architecture, tango, and vibrant culture.

canonical_question: What is the capital of Nigeria?
answer_short: Abuja
answer_detailed: Abuja is the capital of Nigeria, replacing Lagos in 1991. It was chosen as a centrally located, ethnically neutral site. Nigeria is Africa's most populous country with over 220 million people and the continent's largest economy.

canonical_question: What is the capital of Thailand?
answer_short: Bangkok
answer_detailed: Bangkok is the capital of Thailand with over 10 million people. Its official Thai name is the longest place name in the world. It is Southeast Asia's most visited city and a major hub for commerce, transport, and healthcare.

canonical_question: What is the largest lake in the world?
answer_short: Caspian Sea at approximately 371,000 km2
answer_detailed: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake (often called a sea) at approximately 371,000 square kilometres. It is bordered by Russia, Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan, Iran, and Azerbaijan. It is a saltwater lake with no natural outlet to the ocean.

canonical_question: What is the driest place on Earth?
answer_short: Atacama Desert in Chile
answer_detailed: The Atacama Desert in Chile is the driest non-polar place on Earth. Some weather stations there have never recorded rain. Parts of the desert receive less than 1mm of rainfall per year. Despite this, it supports specialized microorganisms.

answer_short: A high-level, general-purpose programming language
answer_detailed: Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language created by Guido van Rossum and released in 1991. Known for its readable syntax and versatility, it is widely used in web development, data science, AI, automation, and scientific computing.

answer_short: HyperText Markup Language, the standard language for web pages
answer_detailed: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard markup language for creating web pages. It defines the structure and content of web pages using tags and attributes. The latest version is HTML5. It works alongside CSS for styling and JavaScript for interactivity.

canonical_question: When was the first computer virus?
answer_short: 1986 (Brain virus)
answer_detailed: The Brain virus, created in 1986 by two Pakistani brothers, is considered the first computer virus for IBM PC compatibles. Earlier self-replicating programs existed, including the Creeper program on ARPANET in 1971, but Brain was the first widespread PC virus.

answer_short: Joseph Nicephore Niepce (first photograph, 1826)
answer_detailed: Joseph Nicephore Niepce produced the first known photograph in 1826/27. Louis Daguerre later developed the daguerreotype process in 1839, making photography practical. George Eastman introduced the Kodak camera in 1888, making photography accessible to the public.

answer_short: Jacob Perkins (first practical model, 1834)
answer_detailed: Jacob Perkins built the first practical refrigerating machine using vapor compression in 1834. Carl von Linde improved the technology in the 1870s. Home refrigerators became common in the 1920s-1930s, revolutionizing food storage and safety.

canonical_question: What is the longest bone in the human body?
answer_short: The femur (thigh bone)
answer_detailed: The femur (thigh bone) is the longest and strongest bone in the human body, typically about one-quarter of a person's height. It connects the hip to the knee and can support up to 30 times the weight of an adult.

canonical_question: How many teeth do adults have?
answer_short: 32 teeth (including wisdom teeth)
answer_detailed: Adults typically have 32 teeth: 8 incisors, 4 canines, 8 premolars, and 12 molars (including 4 wisdom teeth). Children have 20 primary (baby) teeth that are gradually replaced by permanent teeth between ages 6 and 12.

answer_short: Gross Domestic Product
answer_detailed: GDP stands for Gross Domestic Product, the total monetary value of all goods and services produced within a country's borders in a specific time period. It is the primary indicator of a country's economic health. The US has the largest nominal GDP in the world.

answer_short: The SI unit of energy, equal to the work done by a force of one newton over one metre
answer_detailed: A joule (J) is the SI unit of energy. It equals the work done when a force of one newton moves an object one metre. It is named after James Prescott Joule. One calorie equals approximately 4.184 joules. Common multiples include kilojoules (kJ) and megajoules (MJ).

canonical_question: How many grams in a kilogram?
answer_short: 1,000 grams
answer_detailed: There are 1,000 grams in one kilogram. The kilogram is the base unit of mass in the SI system. Since 2019, it is defined by the Planck constant rather than a physical prototype. The prefix kilo means one thousand.

canonical_question: What percentage of the Earth is covered by water?
answer_short: About 71%
answer_detailed: Approximately 71% of Earth's surface is covered by water, with oceans holding about 96.5% of all Earth's water. Only about 2.5% of Earth's water is fresh water, and less than 1% is accessible for human use. The Pacific Ocean alone covers about 30% of Earth's surface.
aliases: how much of Earth is water

canonical_question: How many countries are in the world?
answer_short: 195 (193 UN member states plus 2 observer states)
answer_detailed: There are 195 countries in the world today: 193 member states of the United Nations plus 2 permanent observer states (Vatican City and Palestine). The number changes as new nations form or territories gain independence.

answer_short: The body's framework of bones providing structure and protection
answer_detailed: The skeletal system consists of 206 bones in adults, along with cartilage, ligaments, and tendons. It provides structural support, protects internal organs, enables movement, produces blood cells in bone marrow, and stores minerals like calcium and phosphorus.

answer_short: An essential mineral needed for oxygen transport in blood
answer_detailed: Iron is an essential mineral that helps red blood cells carry oxygen throughout the body as part of hemoglobin. Iron deficiency is the most common nutritional deficiency worldwide, causing anemia. Good sources include red meat, spinach, and legumes.

answer_short: A progressive brain disorder causing memory loss and cognitive decline
answer_detailed: Alzheimer's disease is the most common form of dementia, a progressive brain disorder that slowly destroys memory and thinking skills. It affects about 50 million people worldwide. Named after Alois Alzheimer who first described it in 1906. There is currently no cure.

answer_short: The body's hormone-producing gland system
answer_detailed: The endocrine system consists of glands that produce hormones regulating metabolism, growth, development, reproduction, and mood. Major glands include the pituitary, thyroid, adrenal, and pancreas. Hormones act as chemical messengers carried by the bloodstream.

answer_short: A wireless networking technology using radio waves
answer_detailed: Wi-Fi is a wireless networking technology that uses radio waves to provide high-speed internet and network connections. Based on IEEE 802.11 standards, the term Wi-Fi was coined in 1999. Modern Wi-Fi 6E can reach speeds over 9 Gbps.

answer_short: Central Processing Unit, the brain of a computer
answer_detailed: A CPU (Central Processing Unit) is the primary component of a computer that performs instructions of programs. It executes arithmetic, logic, control, and input/output operations. Modern CPUs contain billions of transistors and operate at gigahertz speeds.

canonical_question: When was the first satellite launched?
answer_short: October 4, 1957 (Sputnik 1)
answer_detailed: Sputnik 1, the first artificial satellite, was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957. It orbited Earth for three months, transmitting radio signals. Its launch marked the start of the Space Age and the US-Soviet space race.

answer_short: Alexander Fleming (1928)
answer_detailed: Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 when he noticed that mold had killed bacteria in a petri dish. Howard Florey and Ernst Chain later developed it into a usable drug in the 1940s. Penicillin became the first widely used antibiotic, saving millions of lives.

answer_short: Karl Benz (1886)
answer_detailed: Karl Benz is credited with inventing the first true automobile powered by an internal combustion engine in 1886. His Benz Patent-Motorwagen is considered the first automobile. Henry Ford later made cars affordable with assembly line production in 1913.

answer_short: F = kx (force is proportional to extension)
answer_detailed: Hooke's law states that the force needed to extend or compress a spring is proportional to the distance of extension. F = kx where k is the spring constant. It applies to elastic materials within their elastic limit.

answer_short: A body immersed in fluid experiences an upward force equal to the weight of displaced fluid
answer_detailed: Archimedes' principle states that any body completely or partially submerged in a fluid at rest is acted upon by an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced. This explains why ships float and hot air balloons rise.

answer_short: The force between two charges is proportional to their product and inversely proportional to the square of their distance
answer_detailed: Coulomb's law describes the electrostatic force between two charged particles. The force is proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. Like charges repel; opposite charges attract.

answer_short: A process where substances transform into different substances
answer_detailed: A chemical reaction is a process where one or more substances (reactants) are converted to one or more different substances (products). Reactions involve breaking and forming chemical bonds. They can be endothermic (absorb energy) or exothermic (release energy).

answer_short: A chemical process involving loss of electrons or addition of oxygen
answer_detailed: Oxidation is a chemical reaction where a substance loses electrons. In everyday terms, it often involves combining with oxygen (like rust forming on iron). Oxidation always occurs with reduction (gain of electrons) in what are called redox reactions.

answer_short: A unit of heredity that codes for a specific protein
answer_detailed: A gene is a sequence of DNA that contains instructions for making a specific protein or set of proteins. Humans have approximately 20,000-25,000 genes. Genes determine inherited traits and are passed from parents to offspring.

answer_short: A linear sequence of organisms where each serves as food for the next
answer_detailed: A food chain is a linear sequence showing how energy and nutrients flow from one organism to another. It starts with producers (plants), then primary consumers (herbivores), secondary consumers (carnivores), and decomposers. Multiple food chains form a food web.
aliases: how does a food chain work

answer_short: An icy body that releases gas and dust when near the Sun
answer_detailed: A comet is a small icy body that, when passing close to the Sun, heats up and releases gases forming a visible atmosphere (coma) and sometimes a tail. Halley's Comet is the most famous, visible from Earth every 75-76 years.

answer_short: A giant cloud of gas and dust in space
answer_detailed: A nebula is a giant cloud of gas and dust in space. Some nebulae are regions where new stars are forming (stellar nurseries), while others are the remnants of dead or dying stars. The Orion Nebula is one of the most famous, visible to the naked eye.

answer_short: Generating electric current by changing magnetic field
answer_detailed: Electromagnetic induction is the production of an electromotive force across an electrical conductor in a changing magnetic field. Discovered by Michael Faraday in 1831, it is the operating principle behind electric generators, transformers, and induction motors.

answer_short: The science of classifying organisms
answer_detailed: Taxonomy is the branch of science concerned with classification of organisms. The modern system uses a hierarchy: domain, kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, species. Carl Linnaeus established the binomial naming system in the 18th century.

answer_short: Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions
answer_detailed: Pascal's law states that a change in pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished to every point of the fluid and to the walls of its container. This principle is the basis for hydraulic systems like brakes and lifts.

answer_short: At constant temperature, gas pressure and volume are inversely proportional
answer_detailed: Boyle's law states that for a fixed amount of gas at constant temperature, pressure and volume are inversely proportional. If you compress a gas to half its volume, the pressure doubles. It is one of the gas laws in thermodynamics.

answer_short: A substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being consumed
answer_detailed: A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without itself being permanently changed or consumed. Catalysts work by providing an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy. Enzymes are biological catalysts.

answer_short: A large molecule made of repeating smaller units
answer_detailed: A polymer is a large molecule composed of many repeated subunits called monomers. Natural polymers include DNA, proteins, and cellulose. Synthetic polymers include plastics (polyethylene, nylon, PVC). Polymerization is the process of forming polymers.

answer_short: The green pigment in plants that captures light energy for photosynthesis
answer_detailed: Chlorophyll is the green pigment found in plants, algae, and cyanobacteria. It absorbs red and blue light while reflecting green light. Located in chloroplasts, it captures light energy and converts it to chemical energy during photosynthesis.

metadata.discoverer: Pierre Joseph Pelletier and Joseph Bienaime Caventou

answer_short: The body's ability to maintain stable internal conditions
answer_detailed: Homeostasis is the tendency of biological systems to maintain relatively constant internal conditions while continuously interacting with and adjusting to external changes. Examples include maintaining body temperature at 37 degrees Celsius and blood pH around 7.4.

answer_short: A streak of light when space debris burns up in Earth's atmosphere
answer_detailed: A meteor is the streak of light produced when a small piece of space debris (meteoroid) enters Earth's atmosphere and burns up due to friction. A meteoroid that survives and hits the ground is called a meteorite. A fireball is an exceptionally bright meteor.

answer_short: A material with electrical conductivity between a conductor and insulator
answer_detailed: A semiconductor is a material whose electrical conductivity falls between that of a conductor and an insulator. Silicon and germanium are common semiconductors. They are the foundation of modern electronics, used in transistors, diodes, LEDs, and computer chips.

metadata.discoverer: William Shockley, John Bardeen, Walter Brattain

answer_short: A device emitting a focused beam of coherent light
answer_detailed: A laser (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation) produces a narrow beam of coherent, monochromatic light through stimulated emission. Invented in 1960, lasers are used in fiber optics, surgery, manufacturing, barcode scanners, and optical storage.

answer_short: The product of all positive integers up to n, written as n!
answer_detailed: A factorial, written as n!, is the product of all positive integers from 1 to n. For example, 5! = 5 x 4 x 3 x 2 x 1 = 120. By convention, 0! = 1. Factorials are used in permutations, combinations, and probability.

answer_short: A mathematical statement comparing two expressions using <, >, <=, or >=
answer_detailed: An inequality is a mathematical relation indicating that one quantity is greater than, less than, or not equal to another. Symbols include < (less than), > (greater than), and their equal variants. Solving inequalities follows similar rules to equations.

answer_short: A number that when multiplied by itself gives the original number
answer_detailed: A square root of a number x is a number y such that y squared equals x. For example, the square root of 9 is 3 because 3 x 3 = 9. Every positive number has two square roots: one positive and one negative. The square root of 2 is irrational.

canonical_question: What is the volume of a cylinder?
answer_short: V = pi r^2 h
answer_detailed: The volume of a cylinder is pi times the radius squared times the height. The surface area is 2*pi*r^2 + 2*pi*r*h. Cylinders appear everywhere from cans to pipes to engines.

canonical_question: What is the volume of a cone?
answer_detailed: The volume of a cone is one-third times pi times the radius squared times the height. A cone has exactly one-third the volume of a cylinder with the same base and height.

canonical_question: What is the Pythagorean theorem for distance?
answer_detailed: The distance formula calculates the distance between two points in a coordinate plane. It is derived from the Pythagorean theorem: d = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2). It extends to 3D with an additional z term.

canonical_question: What is the slope of a line?
answer_short: Rise over run, or m = (y2-y1)/(x2-x1)
answer_detailed: The slope of a line measures its steepness and direction. It equals the change in y divided by the change in x (rise over run). A positive slope goes upward left to right, negative goes downward, zero is horizontal, and undefined is vertical.

canonical_question: What is the order of operations?
answer_short: PEMDAS: Parentheses, Exponents, Multiplication/Division, Addition/Subtraction
answer_detailed: The order of operations is a set of rules for evaluating mathematical expressions. The standard order is PEMDAS: Parentheses first, then Exponents, then Multiplication and Division (left to right), then Addition and Subtraction (left to right).

canonical_question: What is a system of equations?
answer_short: Two or more equations with the same set of variables
answer_detailed: A system of equations is a set of two or more equations sharing the same variables. Solutions satisfy all equations simultaneously. Methods for solving include substitution, elimination, and graphing. A system can have one solution, no solutions, or infinitely many.
aliases: what is a system of equations

answer_short: A formula for the derivative of a composite function
answer_detailed: The chain rule is a formula for computing the derivative of a composite function. If y = f(g(x)), then dy/dx = f'(g(x)) * g'(x). It is one of the most important rules in differential calculus.

answer_short: The formula for the area of a circle
answer_detailed: Pi r squared (A = pi*r^2) is the formula for the area of a circle, where r is the radius. For example, a circle with radius 5 has area 25*pi or approximately 78.54 square units.

answer_short: A measure of how spread out data is from the mean
answer_detailed: Standard deviation measures the amount of variation or dispersion in a set of data values. A low standard deviation indicates values are close to the mean; a high one indicates values are spread out. It is the square root of variance.

answer_short: A mathematical statement that two expressions are equal
answer_detailed: An equation is a mathematical statement asserting the equality of two expressions, connected by the equals sign (=). Equations can be simple (x + 2 = 5) or complex (differential equations). Solving an equation means finding the values that make it true.

answer_short: When a shape looks the same after a transformation
answer_detailed: Symmetry in mathematics means that one shape becomes exactly like another when moved (rotated, reflected, or translated). Types include reflective (mirror), rotational, and translational symmetry. Symmetry is fundamental in geometry, physics, and art.

canonical_question: What is the speed of light constant in physics?
answer_detailed: The speed of light in vacuum (c) is exactly 299,792,458 metres per second. It is a fundamental physical constant used in Einstein's equations and defines the metre in the SI system. Nothing with mass can reach or exceed this speed.

answer_short: An ordered list of numbers following a pattern
answer_detailed: A sequence is an ordered list of numbers that follows a particular pattern or rule. Types include arithmetic sequences (constant difference), geometric sequences (constant ratio), and the Fibonacci sequence. Sequences can be finite or infinite.

answer_short: An arrangement of objects in a specific order
answer_detailed: A permutation is an arrangement of objects in a specific order. The number of permutations of n objects taken r at a time is P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)!. Unlike combinations, the order matters in permutations.

answer_short: A selection of objects without regard to order
answer_detailed: A combination is a selection of items from a collection where the order does not matter. The number of combinations of n objects taken r at a time is C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n-r)!). Unlike permutations, order is irrelevant.

answer_short: A closed plane figure with three or more straight sides
answer_detailed: A polygon is a closed two-dimensional figure made up of straight line segments. Types include triangles (3 sides), quadrilaterals (4), pentagons (5), hexagons (6), and so on. Regular polygons have all sides and angles equal.

answer_short: The study of relationships between angles and sides of triangles
answer_detailed: Trigonometry is the branch of mathematics dealing with relationships between angles and sides of triangles. The main functions are sine, cosine, and tangent. It is essential for physics, engineering, navigation, and computer graphics.

answer_short: A formula for expanding (a+b)^n
answer_detailed: The binomial theorem describes the algebraic expansion of powers of a binomial expression. (a+b)^n = sum of C(n,k)*a^(n-k)*b^k for k=0 to n. It uses binomial coefficients, which form Pascal's triangle.

canonical_question: What is the fundamental theorem of calculus?
answer_short: Links differentiation and integration as inverse operations
answer_detailed: The fundamental theorem of calculus establishes that differentiation and integration are inverse operations. It states that the definite integral of a function can be computed using any one of its antiderivatives. It bridges differential and integral calculus.

metadata.formula: integral(a to b) f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a)

answer_short: A system where numbers wrap around after reaching a certain value
answer_detailed: Modular arithmetic is a system of arithmetic for integers where numbers wrap around upon reaching a certain value (the modulus). For example, in mod 12 (clock arithmetic), 14 mod 12 = 2. It is used in cryptography, computer science, and number theory.

canonical_question: What was the Boston Tea Party?
answer_short: A 1773 political protest against British taxation in Boston Harbor
answer_detailed: The Boston Tea Party on December 16, 1773, was a political protest by American colonists against British taxation without representation. Colonists disguised as Mohawk Indians dumped 342 chests of tea into Boston Harbor. It was a key event leading to the American Revolution.
aliases: when was the Boston Tea Party

answer_short: The largest empire in pre-Columbian America, in South America
answer_detailed: The Inca Empire was the largest empire in pre-Columbian America, stretching along western South America from 1438 to 1533. Based in Cusco (modern Peru), it included about 12 million people. Machu Picchu is its most famous site. Conquered by Spanish conquistador Francisco Pizarro.

answer_short: A 1986 nuclear reactor explosion in Ukraine
answer_detailed: The Chernobyl disaster on April 26, 1986, was the worst nuclear power plant accident in history. Reactor No. 4 at the Chernobyl plant in Ukraine exploded during a safety test, releasing massive amounts of radioactive material. It contaminated large areas of Europe and caused thousands of cancer deaths.

canonical_question: When was the Panama Canal opened?
answer_short: August 15, 1914
answer_detailed: The Panama Canal officially opened on August 15, 1914. The 82 km waterway connects the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans through Panama. Construction took over 10 years and was one of the largest engineering projects ever undertaken, costing about 500 million dollars (1914 value).
aliases: when was the Panama Canal built

canonical_question: When did apartheid end in South Africa?
answer_short: 1994 (first democratic election April 27, 1994)
answer_detailed: Apartheid in South Africa officially ended with the first multiracial democratic election on April 27, 1994, which brought Nelson Mandela to power as the country's first Black president. The system of racial segregation had been in place since 1948.

answer_short: A 1917 revolution that overthrew the Tsar and established the Soviet Union
answer_detailed: The Russian Revolution of 1917 consisted of two main events: the February Revolution (abdication of Tsar Nicholas II) and the October Revolution (Bolshevik seizure of power led by Vladimir Lenin). It led to the creation of the Soviet Union in 1922.

answer_short: Indian independence leader who advocated nonviolent civil disobedience
answer_detailed: Mahatma Gandhi (1869-1948) led India's independence movement against British rule through nonviolent civil disobedience. He organized boycotts, marches (including the Salt March of 1930), and hunger strikes. India gained independence on August 15, 1947. He was assassinated in 1948.

answer_short: Martin Luther's 1517 list of grievances against the Catholic Church
answer_detailed: The 95 Theses were a list of propositions written by Martin Luther in 1517 criticizing the Catholic Church's practice of selling indulgences. Tradition says he nailed them to the door of All Saints' Church in Wittenberg, Germany, sparking the Protestant Reformation.

canonical_question: When was the first human in space?
answer_short: April 12, 1961 (Yuri Gagarin)
answer_detailed: Yuri Gagarin of the Soviet Union became the first human in space on April 12, 1961, orbiting Earth once in the Vostok 1 spacecraft. The flight lasted 108 minutes. This was a major milestone in the Space Race between the US and USSR.

canonical_question: What is the capital of Indonesia?
answer_short: Jakarta (moving to Nusantara)
answer_detailed: Jakarta is the current capital of Indonesia with a metropolitan population of over 30 million. Indonesia is planning to relocate its capital to a new city called Nusantara on the island of Borneo due to Jakarta's severe congestion and flooding issues.

canonical_question: What is the capital of Pakistan?
answer_short: Islamabad
answer_detailed: Islamabad is the capital of Pakistan, replacing Karachi in 1967. It is a planned city built in the 1960s at the base of the Margalla Hills. Pakistan is the fifth most populous country in the world with over 230 million people.

canonical_question: What is the capital of Iran?
answer_short: Tehran
answer_detailed: Tehran is the capital of Iran with a population of about 9 million. Located at the foot of the Alborz mountain range, it has been the capital since 1796. It is the largest city in Western Asia.

canonical_question: What is the capital of Kenya?
answer_short: Nairobi
answer_detailed: Nairobi is the capital and largest city of Kenya with about 4.4 million people. It is a major East African hub for commerce and culture. Nairobi National Park, adjacent to the city, is unique for having wildlife near a major urban center.

answer_short: India, flowing 2,525 km from the Himalayas to the Bay of Bengal
answer_detailed: The Ganges River flows about 2,525 km through India and Bangladesh from the Himalayas to the Bay of Bengal. It is considered sacred in Hinduism. Its basin supports over 400 million people, making it one of the most densely populated river basins in the world.

answer_short: Africa's highest mountain at 5,895 m in Tanzania
answer_detailed: Mount Kilimanjaro is a dormant volcano and the highest mountain in Africa at 5,895 metres (19,341 feet). Located in northeastern Tanzania, it is a free-standing mountain with three volcanic cones. Its glaciers are rapidly shrinking due to climate change.

answer_short: A sea connected to the Atlantic Ocean, between Europe, Africa, and Asia
answer_detailed: The Mediterranean Sea is a body of water between Europe, Africa, and Asia, connected to the Atlantic Ocean through the Strait of Gibraltar. It covers about 2.5 million km2 and is bordered by 21 countries. It has been central to Western civilization for millennia.

answer_short: An imaginary line at 66.5 degrees N latitude
answer_detailed: The Arctic Circle is one of the five major circles of latitude at approximately 66.5 degrees north. Above this line, the sun does not set on the June solstice (midnight sun) and does not rise on the December solstice (polar night). Eight countries have territory within it.

answer_short: An imaginary line at 0 degrees latitude dividing Earth into hemispheres
answer_detailed: The equator is an imaginary line at 0 degrees latitude that divides the Earth into the Northern and Southern hemispheres. It is about 40,075 km long. Countries on the equator include Ecuador, Colombia, Brazil, Congo, Kenya, and Indonesia.

answer_short: Western Europe, flowing through Switzerland, Germany, France, and the Netherlands
answer_detailed: The Rhine River is one of the longest and most important rivers in Europe at about 1,230 km. It flows from the Swiss Alps through Germany and the Netherlands to the North Sea. It is a major waterway for European commerce and trade.

answer_short: A network security system that monitors and controls incoming/outgoing traffic
answer_detailed: A firewall is a network security device that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules. It establishes a barrier between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks like the internet. Types include packet filtering, stateful, and application-level.

answer_short: Computer-generated simulation of a three-dimensional environment
answer_detailed: Virtual reality (VR) is a computer-generated simulation of a 3D environment that can be interacted with using special electronic equipment like headsets and gloves. Applications include gaming, education, medical training, and architectural visualization.

canonical_question: When was the first personal computer?
answer_detailed: The Altair 8800, released in 1975, is often considered the first successful personal computer. The Apple II (1977) and IBM PC (1981) later popularized personal computing. Microsoft and Apple were both founded in this era and transformed the industry.

answer_short: Michael Faraday (1821)
answer_detailed: Michael Faraday created the first electric motor in 1821 by demonstrating electromagnetic rotation. Thomas Davenport built the first practical DC electric motor in 1834. Electric motors now power everything from household appliances to electric vehicles and industrial machinery.

answer_short: Manufacturing objects layer by layer from a digital model
answer_detailed: 3D printing (additive manufacturing) creates three-dimensional objects by depositing material layer by layer based on a digital model. Materials include plastics, metals, ceramics, and even biological cells. Applications span prototyping, medicine, aerospace, and consumer products.

answer_short: John Bardeen, Walter Brattain, and William Shockley (1947)
answer_detailed: The transistor was invented in 1947 at Bell Laboratories by John Bardeen, Walter Brattain, and William Shockley. It replaced vacuum tubes and enabled the miniaturization of electronic circuits, leading to modern computers, smartphones, and all digital technology.

metadata.discoverer: John Bardeen, Walter Brattain, William Shockley

canonical_question: When was the first video game?
answer_short: 1958 (Tennis for Two) or 1972 (Pong commercially)
answer_detailed: Tennis for Two, created by William Higinbotham in 1958, is often cited as the first video game. Spacewar! (1962) was influential in computing. Pong (1972) by Atari was the first commercially successful arcade game, launching the video game industry.

canonical_question: What is the Internet of Things?
answer_short: Network of physical devices connected to the internet
answer_detailed: The Internet of Things (IoT) refers to the network of physical objects embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity to exchange data over the internet. Examples include smart thermostats, wearable fitness trackers, connected cars, and industrial sensors.

answer_short: Elias Howe (1846)
answer_detailed: Elias Howe patented the first practical sewing machine in 1846, using a lockstitch mechanism. Isaac Singer later improved and mass-marketed sewing machines in the 1850s. The sewing machine revolutionized the garment industry and home textile production.

answer_short: Computing using quantum-mechanical phenomena like superposition
answer_detailed: Quantum computing uses quantum-mechanical phenomena such as superposition and entanglement to perform computations. Quantum bits (qubits) can exist in multiple states simultaneously, potentially solving certain problems exponentially faster than classical computers.

canonical_question: What is the population of the world?
answer_short: Approximately 8 billion people
answer_detailed: The world population reached 8 billion on November 15, 2022. It took about 12 years to grow from 7 to 8 billion. India and China are the two most populous countries. The global population growth rate is slowing and is projected to peak around 10.4 billion in the 2080s.

answer_short: The SI unit of power, equal to one joule per second
answer_detailed: A watt (W) is the SI unit of power, equal to one joule of energy per second. Named after James Watt, it measures the rate of energy conversion. A 60-watt light bulb converts 60 joules of electrical energy per second. One kilowatt equals 1,000 watts.

answer_short: It is a Morse code distress signal, not an acronym
answer_detailed: SOS is an international Morse code distress signal (... --- ...) adopted in 1906. Contrary to popular belief, it does not stand for Save Our Souls or Save Our Ship. It was chosen because the Morse code pattern is easy to transmit and recognize.

answer_short: A word or phrase that expresses strong emotion
answer_detailed: An interjection is a word or phrase that expresses a strong emotion or reaction, often standing alone. Examples include 'Wow!', 'Oh no!', 'Ouch!', 'Hooray!', and 'Alas!' They are typically followed by an exclamation mark.

canonical_question: What is an article in grammar?
answer_short: A word that defines a noun as specific or unspecific (a, an, the)
answer_detailed: An article is a word used before a noun to specify its definiteness. English has three articles: 'the' (definite) and 'a/an' (indefinite). 'The' refers to a specific thing; 'a/an' refers to any one of that type. Many languages have more complex article systems.

answer_short: A comparison between two things to highlight a similarity
answer_detailed: An analogy is a comparison between two things that are otherwise unlike, highlighting a specific similarity for explanation or argument. For example, 'Life is like a box of chocolates' or 'The brain is like a computer.' Analogies help explain complex ideas.

answer_short: The art of effective or persuasive speaking or writing
answer_detailed: Rhetoric is the art of effective communication, especially persuasive writing or speaking. It originated in ancient Greece with Aristotle, who identified three modes of persuasion: ethos (credibility), pathos (emotion), and logos (logic).

canonical_question: What is the oldest known language?
answer_short: Sumerian (written records from about 3100 BC)
answer_detailed: Sumerian is the oldest language with written records, dating to about 3100 BC in Mesopotamia. Tamil and Egyptian also have ancient written traditions. The oldest spoken language is debated, as speech predates writing by tens of thousands of years.

answer_short: Indigestible plant material that aids digestion
answer_detailed: Dietary fiber is the indigestible portion of food derived from plants. It comes in two forms: soluble (dissolves in water, lowers cholesterol) and insoluble (does not dissolve, aids bowel movements). Adults should consume 25-30 grams daily. Good sources include whole grains, fruits, and vegetables.

answer_short: An immune system overreaction to a normally harmless substance
answer_detailed: An allergy is an immune system response to a foreign substance (allergen) that is not typically harmful, such as pollen, pet dander, certain foods, or insect stings. Symptoms range from sneezing and hives to life-threatening anaphylaxis. Treatment includes antihistamines and avoidance.

answer_short: A gland that produces insulin and digestive enzymes
answer_detailed: The pancreas is a gland organ located behind the stomach. It has two main functions: producing digestive enzymes (exocrine) and producing hormones including insulin and glucagon that regulate blood sugar (endocrine). Pancreatic dysfunction can cause diabetes.

answer_short: Inflammation of one or more joints causing pain and stiffness
answer_detailed: Arthritis is inflammation of joints causing pain, swelling, and stiffness. The two most common types are osteoarthritis (wear and tear of cartilage) and rheumatoid arthritis (autoimmune attack on joints). It affects over 350 million people worldwide.

canonical_question: What is the longest river in Africa?
answer_short: The Nile River is the longest river in Africa at approximately 6,650 km (4,130 miles). It flows nort...
answer_detailed: The Nile River is the longest river in Africa at approximately 6,650 km (4,130 miles). It flows northward through eleven countries before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

canonical_question: What is the largest hot desert in the world?
answer_short: The Sahara Desert is the largest hot desert in the world, covering approximately 9.2 million square...
answer_detailed: The Sahara Desert is the largest hot desert in the world, covering approximately 9.2 million square kilometers across North Africa. It spans 11 countries.

canonical_question: What is the tallest mountain in North America?
answer_short: Denali (formerly Mount McKinley) in Alaska is the tallest mountain in North America at 6,190 meters...
answer_detailed: Denali (formerly Mount McKinley) in Alaska is the tallest mountain in North America at 6,190 meters (20,310 feet) above sea level.

canonical_question: What is the smallest ocean in the world?
answer_short: The Arctic Ocean is the smallest and shallowest of the five major oceans, covering about 14.06 milli...
answer_detailed: The Arctic Ocean is the smallest and shallowest of the five major oceans, covering about 14.06 million square kilometers. It is located mostly in the Arctic north polar region.

canonical_question: What is the most populous country in the world?
answer_short: India surpassed China as the most populous country in 2023, with over 1.44 billion people. China has...
answer_detailed: India surpassed China as the most populous country in 2023, with over 1.44 billion people. China has approximately 1.43 billion.

canonical_question: What is the deepest lake in the world?
answer_short: Lake Baikal in Siberia, Russia is the deepest lake in the world at 1,642 meters (5,387 feet). It is...
answer_detailed: Lake Baikal in Siberia, Russia is the deepest lake in the world at 1,642 meters (5,387 feet). It is also the oldest lake, estimated at 25 million years old, and holds about 20% of the world's unfrozen surface freshwater.

canonical_question: What is the largest island in the world?
answer_short: Greenland is the largest island in the world with an area of about 2.166 million square kilometers....
answer_detailed: Greenland is the largest island in the world with an area of about 2.166 million square kilometers. It is an autonomous territory within the Kingdom of Denmark.

canonical_question: What is the most active volcano on Earth?
answer_short: Kilauea in Hawaii is often considered the most active volcano on Earth, having erupted almost contin...
answer_detailed: Kilauea in Hawaii is often considered the most active volcano on Earth, having erupted almost continuously from 1983 to 2018. Mount Stromboli in Italy has also been erupting nearly continuously for over 2,000 years.

canonical_question: What are the essential amino acids?
answer_short: There are 9 essential amino acids that the human body cannot synthesize: histidine, isoleucine, leuc...
answer_detailed: There are 9 essential amino acids that the human body cannot synthesize: histidine, isoleucine, leucine, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, threonine, tryptophan, and valine. They must be obtained from food.

canonical_question: What is the difference between Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes?
answer_short: Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune condition where the pancreas produces little or no insulin. Type 2...
answer_detailed: Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune condition where the pancreas produces little or no insulin. Type 2 diabetes occurs when the body becomes resistant to insulin or does not produce enough. Type 2 accounts for about 90-95% of all diabetes cases.

canonical_question: How many bones are in the adult human body?
answer_short: An adult human body has 206 bones. Babies are born with about 270 bones, many of which fuse together...
answer_detailed: An adult human body has 206 bones. Babies are born with about 270 bones, many of which fuse together during growth. The smallest bone is the stapes in the ear.

canonical_question: What is the difference between anxiety and depression?
answer_short: Anxiety is characterized by excessive worry, fear, and nervousness about future events. Depression i...
answer_detailed: Anxiety is characterized by excessive worry, fear, and nervousness about future events. Depression involves persistent sadness, loss of interest, and hopelessness. They can co-occur and share some symptoms like sleep disturbance and difficulty concentrating.

canonical_question: What is BMI and how is it calculated?
answer_short: BMI (Body Mass Index) is calculated by dividing weight in kilograms by height in meters squared: BMI...
answer_detailed: BMI (Body Mass Index) is calculated by dividing weight in kilograms by height in meters squared: BMI = kg/m^2. Categories: underweight (<18.5), normal (18.5-24.9), overweight (25-29.9), obese (30+). It is a screening tool, not a diagnostic measure.

answer_short: Allergies occur when the immune system overreacts to normally harmless substances (allergens) like p...
answer_detailed: Allergies occur when the immune system overreacts to normally harmless substances (allergens) like pollen, dust mites, or certain foods. The body releases histamine and other chemicals, causing symptoms like sneezing, itching, and swelling.

canonical_question: What is the function of red blood cells?
answer_short: Red blood cells (erythrocytes) transport oxygen from the lungs to body tissues and carry carbon diox...
answer_detailed: Red blood cells (erythrocytes) transport oxygen from the lungs to body tissues and carry carbon dioxide back to the lungs. They contain hemoglobin, an iron-rich protein that binds oxygen. Adults produce about 2 million red blood cells per second.

canonical_question: What is the recommended amount of daily exercise?
answer_short: The WHO recommends adults get at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity or 75 minu...
answer_detailed: The WHO recommends adults get at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity or 75 minutes of vigorous activity per week, plus muscle-strengthening activities on 2 or more days per week.

answer_short: Alexander the Great (356-323 BC) was king of Macedon who created one of the largest empires in ancie...
answer_detailed: Alexander the Great (356-323 BC) was king of Macedon who created one of the largest empires in ancient history, stretching from Greece to northwestern India. He was undefeated in battle and died at age 32 in Babylon.

answer_short: The Cold War (1947-1991) was a geopolitical rivalry between the United States and the Soviet Union a...
answer_detailed: The Cold War (1947-1991) was a geopolitical rivalry between the United States and the Soviet Union and their respective allies. It featured an arms race, space race, proxy wars, and ideological competition between capitalism and communism, but no direct military conflict between the superpowers.

answer_short: The Roman Republic (509-27 BC) was the era of ancient Roman civilization when the government operate...
answer_detailed: The Roman Republic (509-27 BC) was the era of ancient Roman civilization when the government operated as a republic with elected officials. It featured the Senate, consuls, and tribunes. It ended when Augustus became the first Roman Emperor.

answer_short: The Crusades were a series of religious wars (1095-1291) sanctioned by the Latin Church, primarily a...
answer_detailed: The Crusades were a series of religious wars (1095-1291) sanctioned by the Latin Church, primarily aimed at recovering the Holy Land from Muslim rule. There were at least eight major Crusades, with the First Crusade (1096-1099) being the most successful.

answer_short: The Industrial Revolution (c. 1760-1840) was the transition from hand production to machine manufact...
answer_detailed: The Industrial Revolution (c. 1760-1840) was the transition from hand production to machine manufacturing, first in Britain. Key innovations included the steam engine, spinning jenny, and power loom. It transformed economies from agrarian to industrial.

answer_short: The Silk Road was an ancient network of trade routes connecting China to the Mediterranean, active f...
answer_detailed: The Silk Road was an ancient network of trade routes connecting China to the Mediterranean, active from the 2nd century BC to the 15th century AD. It facilitated trade in silk, spices, precious metals, and ideas between East and West.

canonical_question: When did World War I begin and end?
answer_short: World War I lasted from July 28, 1914 to November 11, 1918. It was triggered by the assassination of...
answer_detailed: World War I lasted from July 28, 1914 to November 11, 1918. It was triggered by the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand and involved the Allied Powers against the Central Powers. About 17 million people died.

answer_short: Mesopotamia (modern-day Iraq) was a historical region between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers, often...
answer_detailed: Mesopotamia (modern-day Iraq) was a historical region between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers, often called the cradle of civilization. It saw the development of writing (cuneiform), the wheel, mathematics, and the first cities around 3500 BC.

answer_short: The French Revolution (1789-1799) was a period of radical political and societal change in France. I...
answer_detailed: The French Revolution (1789-1799) was a period of radical political and societal change in France. It began with the storming of the Bastille, led to the abolition of the monarchy, and resulted in the rise of Napoleon Bonaparte.

canonical_question: Who was the first person to walk on the Moon?
answer_short: Neil Armstrong was the first person to walk on the Moon on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 missi...
answer_detailed: Neil Armstrong was the first person to walk on the Moon on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 mission. His famous words were: That is one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.

answer_short: The Byzantine Empire (330-1453 AD) was the eastern continuation of the Roman Empire, with its capita...
answer_detailed: The Byzantine Empire (330-1453 AD) was the eastern continuation of the Roman Empire, with its capital at Constantinople (modern Istanbul). It preserved Roman law, Greek culture, and Christianity for over a thousand years until its fall to the Ottoman Turks.

answer_short: The Renaissance (14th-17th century) was a cultural movement that began in Italy, marking the transit...
answer_detailed: The Renaissance (14th-17th century) was a cultural movement that began in Italy, marking the transition from the Middle Ages to modernity. It featured renewed interest in classical learning, advances in art (Leonardo, Michelangelo), science, and literature.

answer_short: The Great Depression (1929-1939) was the worst economic downturn in modern history. It was triggered...
answer_detailed: The Great Depression (1929-1939) was the worst economic downturn in modern history. It was triggered by the stock market crash of October 1929, compounded by bank failures, reduced consumer spending, and poor government policy. Unemployment reached 25% in the US.

answer_short: Cleopatra VII (69-30 BC) was the last active ruler of the Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt. She was known...
answer_detailed: Cleopatra VII (69-30 BC) was the last active ruler of the Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt. She was known for her intelligence, political acumen, and relationships with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony. Her death marked the end of ancient Egypt as an independent state.

answer_short: Onomatopoeia is a word that phonetically imitates or resembles the sound it describes. Examples incl...
answer_detailed: Onomatopoeia is a word that phonetically imitates or resembles the sound it describes. Examples include buzz, hiss, bang, meow, sizzle, and splash.

canonical_question: What is the difference between active and passive voice?
answer_short: In active voice, the subject performs the action (The cat caught the mouse). In passive voice, the s...
answer_detailed: In active voice, the subject performs the action (The cat caught the mouse). In passive voice, the subject receives the action (The mouse was caught by the cat). Active voice is generally preferred for clarity and directness.

canonical_question: What is the most spoken language in the world?
answer_short: By total speakers (native + non-native), English is the most spoken language with about 1.5 billion...
answer_detailed: By total speakers (native + non-native), English is the most spoken language with about 1.5 billion speakers. By native speakers alone, Mandarin Chinese leads with about 920 million native speakers.

canonical_question: What is the difference between an alphabet and a syllabary?
answer_short: An alphabet uses individual characters for consonants and vowels (like Latin or Cyrillic). A syllaba...
answer_detailed: An alphabet uses individual characters for consonants and vowels (like Latin or Cyrillic). A syllabary uses characters that represent whole syllables (like Japanese hiragana and katakana). For example, the syllable ka is one character in a syllabary but two letters in an alphabet.

answer_short: An acronym is a word formed from the initial letters of a phrase, pronounced as a word (NASA, SCUBA,...
answer_detailed: An acronym is a word formed from the initial letters of a phrase, pronounced as a word (NASA, SCUBA, NATO). An initialism is similar but each letter is pronounced separately (FBI, HTML, CEO). Both are types of abbreviations.

canonical_question: What is RAM in a computer?
answer_short: RAM (Random Access Memory) is a computer's short-term volatile memory used for storing data currentl...
answer_detailed: RAM (Random Access Memory) is a computer's short-term volatile memory used for storing data currently being processed. It is much faster than storage drives but loses all data when power is off. Common types include DDR4 and DDR5.

answer_short: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names (like example.com) into IP addresses...
answer_detailed: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names (like example.com) into IP addresses that computers use to communicate. It acts as the internet's phone book, with a hierarchical system of root, TLD, and authoritative name servers.

answer_short: An API (Application Programming Interface) is a set of rules and protocols that allows different sof...
answer_detailed: An API (Application Programming Interface) is a set of rules and protocols that allows different software applications to communicate with each other. REST APIs use HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and are the most common type for web services.

answer_short: Two-factor authentication (2FA) requires two different types of verification to access an account: s...
answer_detailed: Two-factor authentication (2FA) requires two different types of verification to access an account: something you know (password), something you have (phone/token), or something you are (biometric). It significantly reduces the risk of unauthorized access.

answer_short: Version control is a system that tracks changes to files over time, allowing developers to collabora...
answer_detailed: Version control is a system that tracks changes to files over time, allowing developers to collaborate, revert to previous versions, and manage code branches. Git is the most widely used version control system, created by Linus Torvalds in 2005.

answer_short: A GPU (Graphics Processing Unit) is a specialized processor designed for parallel processing of grap...
answer_detailed: A GPU (Graphics Processing Unit) is a specialized processor designed for parallel processing of graphics and mathematical computations. Originally for rendering images, GPUs are now essential for AI/ML training, cryptocurrency mining, and scientific computing.

canonical_question: What is the difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
answer_short: HTTPS (HyperText Transfer Protocol Secure) is the encrypted version of HTTP, using TLS/SSL certifica...
answer_detailed: HTTPS (HyperText Transfer Protocol Secure) is the encrypted version of HTTP, using TLS/SSL certificates to secure data in transit. HTTPS prevents eavesdropping and tampering, and is now standard for all websites. Browsers mark HTTP sites as not secure.

answer_short: Cloud computing delivers computing services (servers, storage, databases, networking, software) over...
answer_detailed: Cloud computing delivers computing services (servers, storage, databases, networking, software) over the internet. Main models: IaaS (infrastructure), PaaS (platform), SaaS (software). Major providers include AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud Platform.

answer_short: Machine learning is a subset of AI where systems learn from data to improve performance without bein...
answer_detailed: Machine learning is a subset of AI where systems learn from data to improve performance without being explicitly programmed. Main types: supervised learning (labeled data), unsupervised learning (pattern discovery), and reinforcement learning (reward-based).

answer_short: A VPN (Virtual Private Network) creates an encrypted tunnel between your device and a remote server,...
answer_detailed: A VPN (Virtual Private Network) creates an encrypted tunnel between your device and a remote server, masking your IP address and securing your internet traffic. It is used for privacy, accessing geo-restricted content, and secure remote work connections.

canonical_question: What is the difference between mitosis and meiosis?
answer_short: Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells with the same chromosome number as the par...
answer_detailed: Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells with the same chromosome number as the parent (diploid), used for growth and repair. Meiosis produces four genetically unique daughter cells with half the chromosomes (haploid), used for producing gametes (sex cells). Meiosis involves two divisions and crossing over for genetic diversity.
